vulns.desc: Unrestricted file upload -> webshell -> RCE. POST /upload (authenticated) saves the file with NO extension/MIME/magic-byte validation. An uploaded template is then rendered server-side by GET /render?doc=<name>, executing {{exec:<cmd>}} -> remote code execution. This is the full upload->webshell->RCE chain; class is 'upload' (RCE is the impact).
vulns.exploit: POST /login {operator/operator} -> token. POST /upload a file 'shell.tpl' with body '{{exec:whoami}}'. GET /render?doc=shell.tpl with the session -> command output in response (RCE).

vulns.desc: Default credentials never rotated: operator/operator grants an authenticated session (the entry point of the upload->RCE chain). Hinted in the / index ('operator onboarding uses the default operator/operator login until rotated').

Path traversal (read) in the download endpoint: GET /files?name=<n> does os.path.join(uploads, name) with no normalization, so ../ escapes the uploads dir and reads arbitrary host files (e.g. the planted server-secrets.txt, /etc/passwd, app source).

Stored XSS via uploaded SVG/HTML: an uploaded .svg/.html is persisted and later served by GET /view?id=<n> with Content-Type text/html, so embedded <script> executes in the victim's browser. Classic upload-driven stored XSS (no output encoding, attacker-controlled content-type).
vulns.exploit: Upload 'x.svg' containing <svg xmlns=...><script>alert(document.domain)</script></svg>. GET /view?id=<id> -> served as text/html, script runs.

vulns.desc: IDOR on uploaded objects: GET /view?id=<sequential int> returns ANY user's uploaded file with no ownership check and no auth required. Enumerate ids to read other users' (incl. admin's) documents.
vulns.exploit: GET /view?id=1 (admin's file) with no session, then id=2,3,... to read everyone's uploads.

vulns.desc: Security misconfiguration (app-wide): debug mode on -> unhandled input returns a full Python traceback in the response body (reproducible e.g. GET /view?id=abc -> ValueError stack trace). Leaks source paths and internals.

Missing security headers across all responses: no Content-Security-Policy, no X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors (clickjacking), no X-Content-Type-Options, no HSTS. Amplifies the stored-XSS and content-type-sniffing issues.
vulns.exploit: curl -I any endpoint -> none of CSP/XFO/XCTO/HSTS present.
